claim__incident_details.manner_first_charged_with_alleged_error:
We were first notified of the alleged error via a certified letter dated 2025-10-14 from the claimants’ attorney, James R. Callahan, Esq. The letter was addressed to my attention, Melissa J. Turner, as the agency’s E&O contact and the producer of record on the underlying homeowners policy. The letter stated that Mr. and Mrs. Peterson were making a formal claim for damages arising out of what they allege was our failure to secure adequate homeowners coverage and specifically our failure to add a scheduled personal property endorsement for high-value jewelry that had been discussed during a coverage review.

Prior to that certified letter, there had been an email exchange on 2025-10-09 from Mr. Peterson to my direct work email, in which he expressed frustration that their insurance carrier had denied coverage for the theft of Mrs. Peterson’s engagement ring and wedding band set following a burglary at their home on 2025-09-28. In that email, he stated that he believed he had requested full coverage for the ring and that he relied on my guidance during our annual review. He asked for an explanation as to why there was no separate jewelry rider on the policy and whether the agency would be responsible for the loss.

After receiving the email, I responded with an explanation of the existing policy limits and the standard sublimit for unscheduled jewelry under their homeowners contract, and I requested copies of the carrier’s denial letter and any appraisals they had. Mr. Peterson then indicated that he would be involving legal counsel. Five days later, we received the formal written demand letter from Attorney Callahan, which clearly alleged negligence and errors and omissions on the part of our agency and specified a monetary demand for reimbursement of the denied loss and associated expenses.

In summary, the first informal notice was by email from the client on 2025-10-09, and the first formal notice and clear allegation of error, accompanied by a specific monetary demand, came by certified mail from their attorney on 2025-10-14. Copies of the email thread and the attorney’s letter have been retained and can be provided as attachments.
claim__incident_details.business_written_direct_or_through_anoth:
Business was written directly by Harborview Insurance Services, LLC as an independent retail agency. We placed the coverage directly with Lakeview Mutual Insurance Company using their online agent portal, and we did not utilize any intermediary wholesale broker or another retail agency. The initial homeowners policy for the Petersons was written in 2021, and the most recent annual coverage review and renewal discussion took place on 2024-11-18 and 2024-11-19 via a combination of phone call and follow-up email. During that review, we discussed several coverage options, including increasing the Coverage A dwelling limit, adjusting their deductible, and potentially scheduling specific items of personal property such as Mrs. Peterson’s engagement ring, a diamond tennis bracelet, and a small collection of antique coins.

I, Melissa J. Turner, conducted the review personally. I explained that unscheduled jewelry under the standard Lakeview Mutual homeowners policy is subject to a sublimit of $5,000 for theft losses, and that any individual item valued above that amount should be itemized and scheduled with its own appraisal to ensure full replacement cost coverage. Mr. Peterson mentioned that they had a recent appraisal for the engagement ring and band set showing a combined value of $18,500. I recall advising him that to fully insure that amount, we would need a copy of the appraisal and a request in writing to add a scheduled personal property endorsement.

My notes from the call indicate that Mr. Peterson said he would scan and email the appraisal later that week, but I did not receive any such documentation. Because we did not receive the appraisal or a written confirmation specifically instructing us to add the jewelry schedule, I did not proceed with binding the endorsement. I did, however, send a follow-up email on 2024-11-19 summarizing the changes we did make to the policy (increasing the dwelling limit and adjusting the deductible) and reiterating that any high-value jewelry would remain subject to the policy’s standard sublimit unless a separate schedule was added.

From the claimants’ perspective, they now state that they believed, based on our discussion, that the ring would be fully covered regardless of whether they sent the appraisal, and that they relied on my professional expertise to ensure that all significant items were properly insured. They are alleging that by not following up more aggressively and by not clarifying in more direct terms that the ring was NOT yet scheduled, our agency failed to procure the requested coverage. Thus, the alleged error centers on the communication and documentation during that direct, retail-agency relationship with Lakeview Mutual and the Petersons, with no involvement from any other brokerage or intermediary in placing or servicing the policy.
